coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated graphitic carbon nitride nanorods for photocatalytic removal of antibiotics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Aili
Lei, Hua
Xi, Fengna
Liu, Jiyang
Qin, Laishun
Chen, Zhi
Dong, Xiaoping
coredata.dc:description: The over-use of antibiotics has resulted in seriously environmental pollution. Metal-free photocatalysts have received tremendous attentions due to their environmental friendliness. Meanwhile, morphology and structure of photocatalysts have significant influence on their photocatalytic performance. Herein, we report a metal-free composite photocatalyst of 0-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated graphitic carbon nitride nanorods (g-CNNR) that was obtained by a hydrothermal treatment. Characterizations of physicochemical properties demonstrate that this GQDs/g-CNNR photocatalyst has a high crystallization level, enhanced visible light absorption and a staggered band alignment, which can promote the formation, the transportation and the separation of photo-excited electrons and holes. These prominent advantages bring improved photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/g-CNNR for efficient removal of antibiotics. Its photocatalytic reaction rate is 3.46 and 2.03 times higher than those of the pristine graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and the g-CNNR, respectively. Furthermore, this composite photocatalyst has good application universality for decomposing other antibiotics, and also possesses excellent stability and reusability. We further proved that photo-induced holes and superoxide radicals are main active species in the photocatalytic process. Our findings suggest that efficient g-C3N4 based photocatalysts can be well fabricated by structural regulation of g-C3N4 and formation of tightly contacted interface between g-C3N4 and GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Graphitic carbon nitride nanorods
Surface modification
Photocatalysis
Removal of antibiotics

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physicochemical properties of GQDs decorated g-CNNR
Photocatalytic performance for degradation of antibiotics
Possible mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of GQDs/g-CNNR photocatalysts.
SEM images of (a) g-CN, (b) g-CNNR and (c, d) GQDs/g-CNNR.

(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM of GQDs; (c) GQDs colloidal suspension under indoor light and UV light (365 nm); (d) Low-magnification and (e) high-magnification TEM images of GQDs/g-CNNR; (f) EDS elemental mapping of C, N, O and P in the GQDs/g-CNNR.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) UV–vis spectra and (d) Tauc plots of different samples.
XPS spectra of g-CN and GQDs/g-CNNR: (a) survey, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s spectra.

Photocatalytic performance of GQDs/g-CNNR for removal of antibiotics: (a) the absorption spectral change; (b) the COD and (c) TOC changes of OTC (the initial concentration: 15 mg L−1); (d) Comparison of photocatalytic degradation ratio of OTC by individual g-CN and g-CNNR, and GQDs/g-CNNR samples with different GQDs weight percentages.
Linear fittings of (a) pseudo-first-order and (b) pseudo-second-order kinetics model for photocatalytic degradation of OTC by various photocatalysts.
(a) The reusability of the optimal GQDs/g-CNNR sample for removal of OTC; (b) The photocatalytic activity of the optimal GQDs/g-CNNR sample for removal of TC.
(a) PL emissions, (b) EIS Nyquist plots, (c) transient photo-current response and (d) MS plots of various samples.

(a) Band alignments of g-CN, g-CNNR and GQDs; (b) Photocatalytic efficiencies of OTC removal by the GQDs/g-CNNR with addition of various quenchers; (c) Possible photocatalytic mechanism of the GQDs/g-CNNR.
Apparent rate constants of photocatalytic OTC degradation from pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated graphitic carbon nitride nanorods for photocatalytic removal of antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, China Jiliang University, 258 Xueyuan Street, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The over-use of antibiotics has resulted in seriously environmental pollution. Metal-free photocatalysts have received tremendous attentions due to their environmental friendliness. Meanwhile, morphology and structure of photocatalysts have significant influence on their photocatalytic performance. Herein, we report a metal-free composite photocatalyst of 0-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated graphitic carbon nitride nanorods (g-CNNR) that was obtained by a hydrothermal treatment. Characterizations of physicochemical properties demonstrate that this GQDs/g-CNNR photocatalyst has a high crystallization level, enhanced visible light absorption and a staggered band alignment, which can promote the formation, the transportation and the separation of photo-excited electrons and holes. These prominent advantages bring improved photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/g-CNNR for efficient removal of antibiotics. Its photocatalytic reaction rate is 3.46 and 2.03 times higher than those of the pristine graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and the g-CNNR, respectively. Furthermore, this composite photocatalyst has good application universality for decomposing other antibiotics, and also possesses excellent stability and reusability. We further proved that photo-induced holes and superoxide radicals are main active species in the photocatalytic process. Our findings suggest that efficient g-C3N4 based photocatalysts can be well fabricated by structural regulation of g-C3N4 and formation of tightly contacted interface between g-C3N4 and GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: As a special group of pharmaceuticals, antibiotics are able to control of human and animal infection diseases, and are widely used in animal husbandry and aquaculture industry [1–3]. However, arbitrary discharge of unconsumed antibiotics and animals’ excretion containing undigested antibiotics has resulted in serious environmental problems [4]. Oxytetracycline (OTC), a tetracycline antibiotic, has been regarded as a worldwide prophylactic antibiotic due to its broad spectrum activity and low cost [5]. Nevertheless, it is suggested that OTC is one of the pharmaceuticals with the highest risks, and even trace amounts of OTC in the natural environment may cause ecosystem turbulence [6,7]. Unfortunately, OTC is non-biodegradable and cannot be sufficiently removed by traditional treating approaches, including biodegradation and chlorination process, because of its hydrophilic properties and stable structure [8,9]. Consequently, it is important to develop more efficient and environmentally friendly technologies to degrade OTC. Photocatalytic technology is a highly effective and green method compared with conventional treatment processes. Thus far, several photocatalysts, such as TiO2, BiOBr and Bi2WO6, have been reported to photocatalytic degradation of OTC [10–12].

In this decade, graphitic carbon nitride g-C3N4 (g-CN), a novel metal-free photocatalyst with physicochemical stability and special optical features, has received numerous attentions, which is extensively used in degradation of various pollutants, hydrogen evolution, and organic synthesis [13–19]. Nevertheless, the practical applications of g-CN are limited due to the high recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. In order to improve the photocatalytic activity of g-CN, many strategies were used such as coupling with other semiconductor, modified with carbon materials, and morphology control [20–29]. Of these methods have been mentioned, coupling with other semiconductor is an effective method not only for separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs but also for broadening the range of visible-light photo-response [24]. In recent years, many g-CN based photocatalysts have been prepared, for instance, γ-Fe2O3/g-CN, SnS2/g-CN, Bi2WO6/g-CN and so on [30–35]. Morphology control is a popular method to improve the photocatalytic activity of g-CN [25,36]. And recently, one-dimensional (1D) carbon nitride nanorods have motivated increasing attract for their high separation efficiency of photo-excited carriers and enhanced photocatalytic performance [37,38].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a novel carbon material present the size of less than 10 nm and are composed of single or few layers of graphene [39,40]. Owing to quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs have potential application in photovoltaics [41,42], bioimaging [43,44], and sensors [45,46]. The application of GQDs in photocatalysis is limited due to the high recombination rate. However, some researchers have found that g-CN modified with GQDs can improve its photocatalytic properties obviously [47–50].

In this paper, GQDs was prepared via a bottom-up method and visible-light-driven GQDs/g-CN nanorods (GQDs/g-CNNR) composites were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal strategy. In comparison with the pristine g-CN and the individual g-CNNR, the GQDs modified g-CNNR has more intensive visible light absorption, low charge recombination rate and high transporting ability of photo-excited carriers. OTC was employed as a model pollutant to investigate the photocatalytic activities of photocatalysts for degrading antibiotics. We studied the effect of GQDs content on the photocatalytic performance, the photocatalytic reaction kinetics, stability, cycling property and universality for other antibiotics. Finally, we proposed a possible photocatalytic mechanism.

Pyrene (>97%) was obtained from Chengdu Cologne chemical reagent company (China). Disodium phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O, 99%), oxytetracycline (OTC, 97%) and tetracycline (TC, 97%) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin industrial corporation (China). Melamine (C3H6N6, 99%) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (China). Other chemicals were obtained from Hangzhou Gaojing Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was prepared from a Millipore Milli-Q purification unit.

The GQDs were synthesized through hydrothermal reaction of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene according to the literature [51,52]. 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (0.06 mmol) and Na2HPO4·12H2O (4.20 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL of NaOH solution (5 mg mL−1) by ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h. Then, the mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 6 h at 200 °C. After the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, the GQDs solution was obtained after removal of large carbon particles by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 30 min.

The pristine g-CN was obtained by thermal polycondensation of melamine, which was reported in our previous literatures [53]. And, the GQDs/g-CNNR photocatalyst was prepared by hydrothermally treating (see Fig. 1 ). Typically, 1 g of g-CN powder and a certain amount of GQDs solution were added to 60 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was further stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 4 h at 150 °C. Finally, the photocatalyst was collected and completely washed by deionized water, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h to further use.

The morphology and elemental distribution of samples were tested by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 250 FEG) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 F) was performed with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The structure of photocatalysts was identified by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a DX-2700 diffractometer (Dandong Haoyuan Instrument Co. Ltd. China). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet Avatar 370 spectrophotometer with a KBr pellet. UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were taken on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–vis spectrometer with an integrating sphere using BaSO4 as the reference. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a VG K-ALPHA instrument using Al Kα monochromatic source. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the materials were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength at 340 nm by Edinburgh FL/FS900 spectrophotometer. Electrochemical performance was characterized by a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China). Total organic carbon (TOC) was measured using a TOC-5000A analyzer (Shimadzu Co. Japan).

OTC was selected to investigate the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts in an aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. The light source is a 300 W xenon lamp (HSX-F300, Beijing NBet) with a 420 nm cutoff filter. For each test, a total of 100 mg of catalyst was dispersed in 100 mL solution of OTC (15 mg mL−1) in a photochemical apparatus. In order to make sure the adsorption equilibrium of solution, the suspension was stirred for 60 min without light. In the photocatalytic reaction process, 4 mL of the aqueous solution was centrifuged and collected every 20 min. The concentrations of OTC, including before and after reaction were analyzed by Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer at wavelength 354 nm. The value of chemical oxygen demand (COD) was measured by a KMnO4 method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of GQDs decorated g-CNNR
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologic evolution of g-CN under hydrothermal treatment was investigated by SEM observation. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the as-prepared g-CN sample has a large and irregular particular shape. After hydrothermally treating it at 150 °C for 4 h, the g-CNNR sample presents a regular rod-like aspect. In fact, the length and diameter of these nanorods are not very uniform. These in Fig. 2b are relatively short and thick, but in some areas they are long and thin (Fig. S1). The obtained GQDs/g-CNNR synthesized by the co-hydrothermal treatment of GQDs and g-CN also exhibits similar nanorod morphology (Fig. 2c and d). These nanorods interlace with each other to form a three-dimensional porous framework that may be favorable for the rapid mass diffusion in photocatalytic reaction. Moreover, GQDs cannot be directly observed due to the small size.

Fig. 3 a displays the TEM image of mono-dispersed GQDs, which have a diameter range of 2–5 nm. Clear crystalline lattices are seen in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 3b), indicating the high crystallization level of GQDs. The estimated basal spacing of ∼0.24 nm is ascribed to the (1120) plane of GQDs [49]. Additionally, the synthesized GQDs suspension is exceptionally stable, and shows a color of brown under indoor light (Fig. 3c). This suggests that GQDs have high utilization efficiency of visible light. However, the photo-generated electrons and holes in GQDs are easy to recombination. It is demonstrated by the intensive green fluorescence emission under UV light radiation. This is the reason that GQDs have a poor photocatalytic activity in published reference [48].

TEM image of the GQDs/g-CNNR is depicted in Fig. 3d, where carbon nitride nanorods with diameter of 300–400 nm and length of several micrometers are revealed. In the high magnification TEM image (Fig. 3e), there are large amounts of porosities on carbon nitride nanorods, whose size is tens of nanometers. That is to say that hierarchically porous structure, including porosities from the nanorod interlacing and pores in nanorods, presents in the GQDs/g-CNNR sample. We also cannot observe GQDs in the samples that should be attributed to the low content of GQDs and the similar phase contract of GQDs and carbon nitride under TEM observation. Nevertheless, via the EDS elemental mapping (Fig. 3f) measurement we can find that the O and P elements in the hydrothermally synthesized GQDs with the presence of P precursor uniformly distribute on carbon nitride. It provides a direct evidence to demonstrate the formation of GQDs/g-CNNR composites. Additionally, the color of GQDs/g-CNNR samples gradually changes from pale yellow to gray with the increase of GQDs content (Fig. S2), also implying the successful modification of g-CNNR with GQDs.

The effect of hydrothermal treatment on the crystal structure of carbon nitride was further studied by XRD technique (Fig. 4 a). The pristine g-CN has two typical diffractions of graphitic carbon nitride with a low crystallization degree that is prepared via thermal condensation of C and N containing precursors [53]. The strong peak at ∼27° corresponds to the interlayer (0 0 2) diffraction and the weak and wide (1 0 0) peak is related to the in-planar ordered array of tri-s-triazine units, respectively. The hydrothermal treatment promotes the crystallization level of g-CNNR, where the (1 0 0) peak becomes sharp compared to g-CN. In the meantime, the g-CNNR sample exhibits some additional peaks at 6.1, 10.6 and 31.0°, further implying the higher crystallinity than g-CN [22,37]. FT-IR spectroscopy was used to analyze the surface functional groups of samples (Fig. 4b). Several absorption bands at 1636, 1458, 1322 and 1248 cm−1 are assigned to the conjugated CN heterocycle, and the sharp peak at ∼810 cm−1 results from the breath mode of tri-s-triazine unit [53]. In addition, the wide band at 3000–3400 cm−1 corresponds to NH and OH stretching vibrations. There is no obvious difference between g-CN, g-CNNR and GQDs/g-CNNR samples, implying that the hydrothermal treatment did not destroy the conjugated CN framework. Similarly, no evident signal of GQDs is also ascribed to its low loading amount.

Results of absorption spectra further confirm the well retaining of carbon nitride skeleton (Fig. 4c). Besides that all the samples have similar spectral shape in the range of 460–200 nm, the absorption edge of carbon nitrides does not apparently shift after the hydrothermal treatment with or without adding GQDs. For the GQDs/g-CNNR, a weak absorption band appears at ∼650 nm that is the GQDs absorption. In comparison with the absorption of colloidal suspension of GQDs, a drastic red-shift is observed for the GQDs in the composite, which may be contributed to the interaction between GQDs and g-CNNR. Fig. 4d illustrates Tauc plots of g-CN and g-CNNR. Their corresponding band gap (E g) values are 2.61 and 2.63 eV.

To confirm the chemistry environment of surface elements, the g-CN and GQDs/g-CNNR samples were characterized using XPS. The three elements of N, C and O are clearly displayed in the survey spectra (Fig. 5 a). Compared with g-CN, the relative content of O significantly increased in the GQDs/g-CNNR, owing to the loaded GQDs and the hydrothermal treatment of g-CNNR [54]. In C 1s spectra (Fig. 5b), two peaks at 284.6 and 288.1 eV are observed, corresponding to sp2 CC/CC bonds and sp2-hybridized C in an aromatic CN ring [48]. An additional peak at 286.9 eV is found in the GQDs/g-CNNR that is related to the CO group. N 1s spectra (Fig. 5c) exhibit three contributions at 401.1, 399.9 and 398.7 eV, which are attributed to NH groups, tertiary nitrogen N(C)3 groups linking structural motif and CN-C groups, respectively [48]. The increased content of N(C)3 groups confirms that the GQDs/g-CNNR has a high crystallinity. For O 1s spectra (Fig. 5d), the prominent signal in the GQDs/g-CNNR is deconvoluted into two peaks. The 532.9 eV absorption is attributed to CO units and the peak at 532.4 eV results from CO groups. Additionally, P element is not checked because of the low GQDs content in GQDs/g-CNNR as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance for degradation of antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: We employed OTC as a model antibiotic to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the GQDs decorated g-CNNR sample. Fig. 6 a is the spectral change of OTC solution (15 mg L−1) with the presence of GQDs/g-CNNR with a GQDs mass percentage of 0.75% under visible light irradiation. The surface adsorption of OTC on the photocatalyst rapidly reaches equilibrium in dark that is indicated by the unchanged absorption spectra with the 20 min interval. Once starting the visible light illumination, the photocatalytic decomposition of OTC is immediately triggered, and the absorption band centered at 354 nm gradually decreases. Meanwhile, a new absorption appears in the range of 400–500 nm, which is related to the produced intermediate. Also, this additional absorption step-by-step diminishes with the increase of irradiation time. To reveal the mineralization of OTC, we examined the COD and TOC values of OTC solution before and after photocatalytic reaction, and the results are listed in Fig. 6b and c. The initial COD value of OTC solution is 15.97 mg O2 L−1. After 120 min irradiation, the value decreases to 6.96 mg O2 L−1, and the calculated COD removal efficiency is 56.4%. Accordingly, the photocatalytic reaction results in the decrease of TOC value from 9.65 mg L−1 to 3.08 mg L−1, and the TOC removal efficiency is 68.1%. Different concentrations of OTC were also used to evaluate the photocatalytic behavior of the GQDs/g-CNNR composite (Fig. S3). When using 10 and 15 mg L−1 OTC concentrations, similar photocatalytic performances are obtained. However, as further increasing the OTC concentration, the OTC removal efficiency gradually declines. Thus, we employed a 15 mg L−1 OTC solution to further study the photocatalytic property of GQDs/g-CNNR composite.

Fig. 6d shows the photocatalytic decomposition ratio of OTC by g-CN, g-CNNR and GQDs modified g-CNNR samples with varied GQDs mass percentages. The pristine g-CN exhibits gradually decreased C/C0 values and finally obtains a value of ∼0.47 after 120 min. Compared to g-CN, the photocatalytic activity of the g-CNNR is greatly enhanced, which presents a ∼70% degradation ratio at 120 min. We ascribe this result to its hierarchical porous structure, as well as its high crystallization level that is favorable for the transportation of photo-generated electrons and holes. The photocatalytic performance is further improved by modifying GQDs on the surface of g-CNNR because of the enhanced visible light absorption and the promotion of photo-excited charge separation. Among these GQDs/g-CNNR samples, the photocatalyst of 0.75% GQDs content has the highest photocatalytic activity. In the first 20 min photocatalytic process, the concentration of OTC rapidly reduces, and a ∼50% efficiency is gotten that finally is ∼80% after 120 min.

We investigated the kinetics of photocatalytic OTC degradation by GQDs/g-CNNR photocatalysts using the pseudo-first-order (Eq.1) and pseudo-second-order (Eq.2) models as follow:
(1)
l n
C 0
C
=
k 1
t
(2)
1 C
-
1
C 0
=
k 2
t
where t is the irradiation time, C 0 and C are the OTC concentrations at the reaction time of 0 and t min, k 1 is the apparent rate constant of pseudo-first-order kinetics and k 2 represents the apparent rate constant of pseudo-second-order kinetics. The linear fitting data of these two kinetics models are displayed in Fig. 7 and the corresponding apparent rate constants are listed in Table 1 . The correlation coefficients (R 2) demonstrate that the pseudo-second-order model is fitted better to describe the photocatalytic process of OTC removal than the pseudo-first-order model. It is clearly observed that the GQDs/g-CNNR with 0.75% GQDs content has a largest rate constant that is 3.46 and 2.03 times higher than those of g-CN and g-CNNR, respectively.

The stability and reusability of the optimal GQDs/g-CNNR was investigated by the successive 5 cycles of photocatalytic experiments for removal of OTC. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, the photo-degradation efficiency of OTC has no evident decrease, and the values of five cycles are all between 70% and 80%. In the meantime, the performance of the GQDs/g-CNNR for decomposing other antibiotic, tetracycline (TC), was also tested. Fig. 8b is the UV–vis spectra of TC solution (15 mg L−1) at different irradiation time. As prolonging the irradiation time, the intensity of its absorption peak at ∼354 nm rapidly decrease, and after 120 min illumination the removal efficiency is ∼80% (inset of Fig. 8b). These results demonstrate that this photocatalyst has high stability and good universality for removing antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the reason for improved activity of the GQDs/g-CNNR, we studied the charge separation and transfer ability in these photocatalysts. Fig. 9 a presents PL emission spectra of g-CN, g-CNNR and GQDs/g-CNNR samples under the 340 nm excitation. The emission intensity implies the recombination rate of photo-produced electrons and holes. It is apparently found that the g-CN has the largest PL intensity. The crystallization level is enhanced by the hydrothermal treatment that promotes the charge transportation in the g-CNNR and therefore results in a much lower PL signal than g-CN. The PL intensity further weakens as decorating GQDs on the surface of g-CNNR, which is attributed to the enhanced transfer and separation rate of photo-induced electrons and holes. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a powerful tool to analyze the charge transfer resistance between reactants and photocatalysts, as well as the conductivity of photocatalysts. Fig. 9b depicts EIS Nyquist plots of g-CN, g-CNNR and GQDs/g-CNNR, where the diameter of semi-cycle denotes the value of charge transfer resistance. Among these photocatalyst, the GQDs/g-CNNR has the smallest resistance, indicating its highest conductivity and transfer ability of charges on the interface. The highest separation efficiency and the best migration capability of GQDs/g-CNNR among these photocatalysts are also demonstrated by its largest transient photo-current response (Fig. 9c).

Electrochemical Mott-Schottky (MS) measurement was employed to determine the semiconductive type and the flat band potential of photocatalysts. We can see from Fig. 9d that all these MS curves have positive slopes, implying they are all n-type semiconductors. Thus, the flat band potential can be regarded as the conduction band minimum (CBM). The estimated values of flat band potentials are −1.12 V, −1.03 V and −1.22 V vs. Ag/AgCl for g-CN, g-CNNR and GQDs, respectively.

According to the band gaps of g-CN and g-CNNR (Fig. 4d) and the gap of GQDs (Fig. S4), we plotted the band alignments of g-CN, g-CNNR and GQDs in Fig. 10 a. GQDs and g-CNNR present the staggered band structure, and therefore form an n-n type heterojunction catalyst. GQDs with a small band gap can absorb more visible light to produce photo-generated electrons and holes. These photo-produced electrons would be subsequently transferred to the CB of g-CNNR. In photocatalytic process, there are three mainly possible active species, including photo-excited holes (h+), hydroxyl (·OH) radicals from the oxidation of water by holes and superoxide (
O2 −) radicals that are formed by the reduction of adsorbed O2 by photo-induced electrons. To prove the main active species, the effect of adding various quenchers, 6 mM (EDTA-2Na) for h+, 6 mM (TBA) for
OH and 0.5 mM (BQ) for
O2 −, on the photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/g-CNNR was studied and the results are shown in Fig. 10b. Compared to the test without addition of scavengers, the photocatalytic efficiencies after adding EDTA-2Na and BQ drastically decrease. However, as adding TBA, the activity has no obvious change. These results suggest that photo-generated holes and
O2 − are main active species in the photocatalytic process of degrading OTC by the GQDs/g-CNNR.

Based on the experimental results that have been discussed above, the photocatalytic mechanism of GQDs/g-CNNR is proposed, as seen in Fig. 10c. The visible light irradiation forms photo-generated electrons and holes, which are respectively accumulated on the CB and VB of GQDs and g-CNNR. The high crystallization level of g-CNNR promotes the charge transportation rate, and thus more photo-induced charges can diffuse to the photocatalyst surface before recombination. Furthermore, due to the staggered band alignment in the type II GQDs/g-CNNR heterojunction, electrons on the CB of GQDs rapidly transfer to the CB of g-CNNR, and meanwhile holes on the VB of g-CNNR transport to the VB of GQDs. The collected electrons on the CB of g-CNNR are able to react with adsorbed oxygen to form
O2 − radicals. Accordingly, these active species of photo-generated holes and
O2 − radicals with powerful oxidation capability can directly decompose OTC into small molecules.

In summary, we reported a simple and facile strategy to synthesize a metal-free composite photocatalyst of GQDs decorated graphitic carbon nitride nanorods. The content of GQDs had great influence on the photocatalytic activity of composite photocatalyst, and the optimum GQDs/g-CNNR (with the content of 0.75% GQDs) presented ∼80% for OTC removal after 2 h and the corresponding COD and TOC removal respectively reached at 56.4% and 68.1%, which demonstrated photocatalysis was one of the effective methods of antibiotics degradation. The obtained GQDs/g-CNNR photocatalyst exhibited much higher photocatalytic performance than g-CN and g-CNNR under visible light. The reason for the excellent photocatalytic performance could be ascribed to high utilization efficiency of visible light and the significantly enhanced transportation and migration rate of photo-generated charges. Additionally, the GQDs/g-CNNR possessed excellent reusability and good universality for multiple antibiotics. This well designed metal-free composite photocatalyst by coupling two carbonaceous materials and regulating the morphology as well provides an effective and environmentally friendly strategy to remove antibiotics from environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported from the National Key Research Program of China (SQ2017YFGH001487), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY17B010004, LY17E020008) and the 521 talent project of ZSTU.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.04.027.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Promoters, enhancers, and transcription target RAG1 binding during V (D) J recombination
Facile construction of novel direct solid-state Z-scheme AgI/BiOBr photocatalysts for highly effective removal of ciprofloxacin under visible light exposure: mineralization efficiency and mechanisms
